three-sisters-soup.title: The Three Sisters
three-sisters-soup.script: The Three Sisters—corn, beans, and squash—have been grown together by Haudenosaunee and many other Indigenous nations for centuries. Corn provides a pole for beans to climb; beans add nitrogen to the soil; squash leaves shade the ground and keep moisture. Together they create a sustainable system that fed communities and taught balance with the land.

three-sisters-stew.title: The Three Sisters
three-sisters-stew.script: The Three Sisters—corn, beans, and squash—have been grown together by Haudenosaunee and many other Indigenous nations for centuries. Corn provides a pole for beans to climb; beans add nitrogen to the soil; squash leaves shade the ground and keep moisture. Together they create a sustainable system that fed communities and taught balance with the land.

bannock.title: The Story of Bannock
bannock.script: Bannock is a staple bread for many First Nations, Métis, and Inuit peoples. Its history is tied to the introduction of flour through trade and later government rations. Communities adapted the recipe with what they had and made it their own. Today bannock is a symbol of resilience and gathering, often shared at feasts and with family.

wojapi.title: Wojapi and the Berries
wojapi.script: Wojapi is a traditional berry sauce or pudding from Lakota and Dakota peoples. Berries were gathered from the land and cooked down. The dish connects people to the seasons and to the plants that have sustained communities for generations.

wild-rice.title: Manoomin — Wild Rice
wild-rice.script: Manoomin, or wild rice, is sacred to Anishinaabe and other peoples of the Great Lakes. It grows in shallow waters and is traditionally harvested by hand from a canoe. The grain has fed nations for thousands of years and is central to ceremonies and identity.

pemmican.title: Pemmican
pemmican.script: Pemmican was developed by Plains Cree, Métis, and other Indigenous peoples as a high-energy food that could last for months. Dried meat and fat were pounded together, often with dried berries. It sustained hunters, travelers, and families through long winters and long journeys.

salmon-cedar.title: Cedar and Salmon
salmon-cedar.script: Coastal First Nations have long cooked salmon on cedar planks over an open fire. Cedar gives the fish a gentle, smoky flavour and honours the tree that provides so much. Salmon is a keystone of life and culture on the coast.

berry-bowl.title: Berry Bowl
berry-bowl.script: Fresh berries have been gathered and shared by Indigenous peoples across Turtle Island for generations. Served with honey or maple, a berry bowl connects us to the land and to the seasons when each fruit is ready.

apple-berry-sauce.title: Apple or Berry Sauce
apple-berry-sauce.script: Cooked fruits with honey or maple have long been part of Indigenous foodways. Apples and berries were preserved for the winter and shared at gatherings. This simple dish carries forward the practice of using what the land offers.

_default.title: Indigenous Food Traditions
_default.script: This dish is part of a long tradition of Indigenous cooking across Turtle Island. Indigenous peoples have always adapted to the land and the seasons, using what was available and sharing knowledge between nations. Today, Indigenous chefs and communities continue to honour these foods and the stories they carry.
